frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
generated_comment: Suniye, mujhe Ritu ki chinta ho rahi hai. Pehle hi din se — Rahul ne yeh kaha, Amit ne woh kiya, sab saath chai peene gaye. Itni jaldi itni ghulmil gayi inse? Ladkiyon ko thoda sambhal ke rehna chahiye, log kya sochenge. Aap hi baat karein usse, meri baat woh sunegi nahi. Humari beti ka naam kharab ho, yeh hum nahi chahte.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Kamla frames Ritu's normal workplace socializing with male colleagues as a reputational risk and character concern, implying that a woman who is comfortable around men is inviting negative judgment — a classic double standard where female sociability is read as moral looseness. The misogyny is covert because it is expressed as familial concern and reputation management rather than explicit condemnation.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 22 has just started her first job. She comes home talking about her male colleagues Ñ Rahul said this, Amit did that, they all went for chai after work. You are listening. You are not saying anything in the moment. But you are sitting with your husband that night and telling him what you have been hearing and what you think about a girl who is new to a job and already so comfortable with so many boys and what this kind of comfort leads to and what needs to be said to her.
